Pull your bottom heel toward your glute to stretch your quad. This multi-planar stretch mobilizes your thoracic spine, shoulders, hips, and quads simultaneously. Hold and breathe diaphragmatically, allowing the nervous system to relax into this deep twist posture.

Stand tall on a raised box or flat ground, feet together. Lock your knees completely. Inhale, brace lightly, and tuck your chin to your chest. Slowly round your spine forward, segment by segment, starting from your neck, then upper back, mid-back, and lower back. Let your arms hang heavy toward the floor. Go as deep as possible under control, feeling a deep stretch along your entire posterior chain—from your calves up to your neck. Hold the bottom position, taking a slow diaphragmatic breath, allowing the spine to decompress. Roll up slowly, reversing the movement, segment by segment, until you are standing tall. This decompresses the vertebral column.

Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Let your body hang completely free, keeping your arms straight and shoulders relaxed. Squeeze your legs together lightly, but let your hips, pelvis, and spine hang completely passive. Inhale deeply into your lower abdomen, feeling your ribcage expand. On a slow, relaxed exhale, let gravity pull your pelvis down, decompressing your lumbar vertebrae and elongating your spine. You will feel a deep release in your lower back, lats, and shoulders. Hold this position, breathing slowly. This dead hang provides passive traction, reducing pressure on spinal discs and down-regulating spinal erectors.

Anchor a heavy resistance band to a stable post behind you. Loop the other end around the front of your ankle joint, resting on the talus bone. Step forward to create heavy forward tension. Place your foot flat on a step or floor. Inhale, brace, and drive your knee forward over your toes, keeping your heel firmly planted. The band's backward pull distracts the ankle joint, creating joint space and overcoming bony restrictions. Hold the forward position for three seconds, then release. Repeat this mobilization slowly, focusing on deep breathing. This protocol dramatically increases ankle dorsiflexion, which is critical for knee and hip mechanics.

Assume a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Rotate your hands so the backs of your hands are flat on the floor, fingers pointing toward each other. Spread your fingers wide. Pull your shoulders down away from your ears, stabilizing your scapulae. Inhale, brace lightly, and slowly shift your weight from side to side, then rock back slightly. Keep your elbows locked and the backs of your hands flat; do not let them peel up. You will feel a deep, passive stretch in your wrist extensors and forearms. Hold and breathe slowly, allowing the joint capsule to decompress. This joint prep restores wrist extension and relieves carpal tunnel stress.

Drape a mobility strap or heavy towel over your shoulder, crossing it over your upper trap. Grasp both ends with your hand, pulling downward and forward with firm pressure to pin the first rib. Tilt your head back slightly and tilt it away from the strapped side. Inhale deeply into your upper chest, feeling the rib try to push against the strap's pressure. On a slow, controlled exhale, allow your head to sink deeper into the stretch, releasing the scalenes and opening the thoracic outlet. Hold this position, focusing on slow diaphragmatic breathing. This release reduces shoulder shrugging, neck stiffness, and thoracic outlet compression.

Lie flat on your back, raising one leg to ninety degrees and grasping behind your knee with both hands. Keep your opposite leg straight and flat on the floor. Inhale, brace your core, and slowly extend your working knee, pointing your toes toward the ceiling. Extend only until you feel a mild stretch in your hamstring. Hold this peak position for two seconds, then slowly lower your foot. Repeat this movement slowly and dynamically, flossing the sciatic nerve and hamstring muscle belly. Focus on slow diaphragmatic breathing, keeping your pelvis flat on the floor. This dynamic mobilization increases hamstring length without triggering the stretch reflex.

Anchor a heavy resistance band to a stable post. Loop the other end around your upper thigh, close to your groin. Step away from the post, creating a heavy lateral and forward pull. Kneel on your inside knee, stepping your outside foot forward into a lunge. Inhale deeply, and as you exhale slowly, let your hips sink forward, allowing the band to pull your femur laterally. This distraction creates space inside the hip joint capsule, reducing impingement and mobilizing the posterior capsule. Hold this position, breathing diaphragmatically. Let your hips sink deeper on each exhale. This restores hip extension and internal rotation.

Stand tall, crossing your working leg behind your opposite leg, keeping both feet flat on the floor. Place your hand on a wall or a stable support for balance. Inhale deeply, lengthening your entire spine toward the ceiling. On a slow, controlled exhale, push your hips laterally toward the working side. Keep your torso completely straight—do not lean forward, slump, or twist your pelvis. Feel the deep stretch down your outer thigh and hip, specifically targeting the tensor fasciae latae and the iliotibial band. Hold this position, focusing on deep diaphragmatic breathing into your lateral ribcage and hip. On each exhale, allow your hips to sink further laterally, releasing tension under passive traction. This reduces hip tightness and lateral knee stress.

Stand tall with your feet hip-width apart, arms fully extended at your sides, hands in tight fists. Squeeze your glutes, lock your knees, and brace your core to prevent your spine or neck from moving. Now, perform Controlled Articular Rotations (CARs) with your shoulder blades. Slowly elevate your shoulders toward your ears. Retract them, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Depress them, pulling them down into your back pockets. Protract them, pushing them forward as far as possible. Complete this slow, circular path under strict control, isolating the movement to your scapulae. Focus on slow, diaphragmatic breathing. This restores scapular mobility and motor control.

Assume a quadruped position on your hands and knees. Keep your spine flat and core braced. Lift one knee off the ground, keeping it bent at ninety degrees. Perform Hip Controlled Articular Rotations (CARs). Slowly flex your hip, bringing your knee toward your chest. Abduct your hip, lifting your knee out to the side like a dog at a fire hydrant. Internally rotate your hip, kicking your heel toward the ceiling while keeping your knee high. Extend your hip, kicking straight back, then return to the start. Complete this slow circle, avoiding any spinal movement or pelvic tilt. This maintains hip joint health and cartilage.

Stand tall with your feet hip-width apart, hands in tight fists at your sides. Squeeze your glutes, lock your knees, and brace your core to isolate your neck. Perform Neck Controlled Articular Rotations (CARs). Slowly tuck your chin to your chest. Rotate your chin toward your right shoulder. Tilt your right ear back toward your shoulder. Extension your neck, tracing your chin across the ceiling. Tilt your left ear toward your left shoulder. Rotate your chin to your left shoulder, then tuck your chin back to your chest. Complete this slow circle under control, avoiding any shoulder movement. This maintains cervical spine joint health.

Assume a quadruped position on your hands and knees, with your hands placed directly under your shoulder joints and your knees under your hips. Your spine should start in a completely flat, neutral position. Focus on mobilizing your vertebral column segment by segment, starting from your pelvis. Inhale deeply into your abdomen. On a slow, controlled exhale, tilt your pelvis anteriorly, allowing your lower back to arch. Slowly ripple this extension up your spine, vertebra by vertebra, ending by lifting your gaze. Now, inhale deeply, and as you exhale, tuck your pelvis posteriorly, rounding your lower back. Sequentially move this flexion up your spine, segment by segment, ending by tucking your chin to your chest. This segmental motor control down-regulates spinal erectors and reduces muscular guarding.
